summary: Daphne Koller discusses how her company, Insitro, uses machine learning to revolutionize drug discovery. She advocates for an 'anti-hypothesis driven' approach that leverages massive datasets from human genetics and cellular systems to uncover biological patterns that are too complex for the human brain to comprehend.

principles.statement: Drug discovery should be anti-hypothesis driven.
principles.rationale: Traditional hypothesis-driven drug discovery has a 90% failure rate because human biology is too complex to understand manually. Instead, we should collect massive amounts of unbiased data and let machine learning surface the hypotheses.
principles.source_quote: We take a very different approach that is anti-hypothesis driven... rather than starting out with a hypothesis and then doing experiments to test those out we do experiments to feed into machine learning models who tell us what the interesting findings the interesting insights that emerge from those data

principles.statement: The human brain cannot comprehend the complexity of human biology.
principles.rationale: We define diseases using coarse-grained, subjective clinical symptomology and try to intervene in complex systems we don't truly understand, which leads to massive clinical trial failures.
principles.source_quote: I believe that the the challenges that the human brain just cannot understand the human biology and it's in its complexity it's just a very challenging problem

principles.statement: Cross-disciplinary cultures must be deliberately architected.
principles.rationale: Life scientists and computational scientists naturally use different jargon and talk past each other. If you don't deliberately build a culture where they form a single team with a shared vocabulary, the collaboration will fail.
principles.source_quote: if you create a world in which those people are actually part of a team they develop a shared vocabulary and a shared Vision magic just happens and they start kind of building on each other in ways because they come from such orthogonal perspectives where the whole is so much larger than the sum of the parts

principles.statement: Open the 'walled gardens' of data to researchers.
principles.rationale: While patient privacy must be protected, keeping data locked away to only the original creators stifles innovation. Allowing qualified researchers into protected environments unleashes a torrent of innovation.
principles.source_quote: creating these protected research environments these wild Gardens that allow people from all over to Common and uncover new insights is I think really really important

frameworks.name: Bridging Data Modalities for Causal Predictions
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building predictive models of causal interventions in humans, where direct causal experimentation on humans is impossible or unethical.
steps: Collect human genetic and complex phenotypic data to establish a ground truth for causality (since genetics drive phenotypes in one direction).
Generate in-house cellular data using human-derived cells and genetic interventions (like CRISPR) to observe how cells change state.
Use machine learning to create meaningful representations of both high-content data modalities.
Bridge the two modalities using ML so that causal experiments in the lab truly predict the effect of an intervention in a human being.
frameworks.source_quote: we've taken a rather unusual strategy of bringing together and bridging between two very distinct and complementary data modalities

mental_models.name: Drug Discovery as a Forked Road
mental_models.description: Viewing the drug development lifecycle not as a straight pipeline, but as a series of forks in the road. The high cost of drug discovery comes from taking the wrong forks, leading to costly detours or dead ends. Machine learning acts as a predictive compass at these forks.
mental_models.example: Using ML to predict which target and patient population to select before entering Phase 2 clinical trials, thereby avoiding the 'wrong fork' that causes most drugs to fail.

mental_models.name: Language Models of Biology
mental_models.description: Treating massive biological datasets (like histopathology images, cellular morphologies, and transcriptomes) as a 'language' that machine learning can read and interpret, similar to how NLP models process text.
mental_models.example: Applying unbiased machine learning to histopathology slides to discover that the tumor microenvironment is a significant prognostic factor for survival, an insight previously missed by human pathologists.

heuristics: Do experiments to feed into machine learning models, not to test human hypotheses.
Stand on the shoulders of giants from different disciplines to create unique insights.

signature_quotes.text: The human brain just cannot understand the human biology and its complexity.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why traditional drug discovery has a 90% failure rate.

signature_quotes.text: We take a very different approach that is anti-hypothesis driven.
signature_quotes.context: Describing Insitro's core methodology of letting hypotheses emerge from unbiased data.

signature_quotes.text: Each successful drug carries on its back the cost of all of those failures.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why the average cost of bringing a drug to market is in the billions.

signature_quotes.text: I'm not sure the one that came from the AI is any less valid than the one that came from the brain of a scientist.
signature_quotes.context: Addressing AI safety concerns in drug discovery, noting that clinical trials vet all hypotheses equally.

signature_quotes.text: As soon as you unleash scientists onto a large Rich data set magic begins to happen.
signature_quotes.context: Advocating for opening up protected health data environments to more researchers.

anti_patterns.description: Hypothesis-driven drug discovery based on small model systems and preconceived clinical notions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It relies on the human brain to understand systems that are too complex, leading to a 90% failure rate when the drug finally reaches clinical trials (especially Phase 2) because the target doesn't actually modulate the clinical outcome.

anti_patterns.description: Siloing computational scientists and life scientists in an organization.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: They use different jargon and think about the world in different ways, causing them to talk past each other rather than compounding their expertise.

anti_patterns.description: Implementing a six-month moratorium on AI development.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It only stops law-abiding organizations, putting them six months behind less scrupulous actors who will not pause their development.
